data.title: Buy The Dip On Bargain Dividends Before Others Do
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

BDCs (Business Development Companies) have thrived in the wake of the Great Financial Crisis. They are a type of fund that was created in 1980, when Congress made amendments to the Investment Act of 1940. Yet, before the 2000s, they weren't particularly large and weren't publicly traded.

BDCs fall into a category of investments known as RICs (Regulated Investment Company). Other types of RICs include REITs, CEFs (Closed-End Funds), Mutual Funds, and ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds) – all of which derive their regulatory framework from the Investment Act of 1940 and the amendments that have occurred since.

The main benefit of RICs is that they get favorable tax treatment. They don't have to pay tax on the bulk of their taxable income in exchange for following a series of regulatory requirements and distributing most of their taxable income to shareholders. As a result, these investments typically pay minimal amounts of taxes at the corporate level and pass along significant dividends to investors. The dividends are then taxable to shareholders. Under the Big Beautiful Bill, BDC dividends can now qualify as Section 199a dividends starting in tax year 2026, making the treatment of BDC dividends more similar to how REITs are currently treated and making BDCs slightly more tax-efficient.

One might think this would make BDCs more popular; however, BDC prices have generally trended downward.

Credit Risk

BDCs sold off after the First Brands bankruptcy as market fears surrounding credit risk increased. On the one hand, this makes sense because BDCs are in the business of lending to businesses that are not publicly traded, like First Brands.

Most BDCs are deeply tied to private equity and are often externally managed by private equity. As a result, many of their loans are with companies that are "sponsored" by private equity. The PE company provides the equity, and the BDC provides loans.

What makes BDCs different from banks is that they have the ability to be very flexible with their debt structures. It is common for BDCs to accept preferred equity or warrants in the common equity as partial compensation for a loan. Loans might have features like a PIK (payment-in-kind) toggle, where the company can pay some of the interest due in additional principal or stock. While most BDCs focus on senior-secured loans, most will carry some level of subordinated loans, preferred equity, or asset-based loans. This makes them very flexible lenders that can tailor a debt package that fits the needs and practical realities of a business.

While BDCs can be flexible, ultimately, every lender relies on most of their borrowers to repay as agreed. Defaults on debt often result in credit losses, which lower total return. So when defaults are in the news, some investors just sell all credit risk investments.

Yet reality doesn't always match the headlines. As BDCs reported Q3 earnings, it turned out that credit quality remained strong and non-accruals remained low relative to history. There is little evidence that we are seeing a real credit crisis.

The Real Headwinds

BDCs are facing some headwinds. Most notable is that many BDCs hold floating-rate assets and have fixed-rate liabilities. As a result, rising interest rates were very positive for cash flow. Now, we are seeing earnings come back down to Earth as interest rates come back down. Distributions will decline – that is inevitable, and it is a reality. We can expect that earnings are going to generally trend downward, and that would happen even if interest rates remain high because their fixed-rate bonds issued in 2020-2022 will mature and have to be refinanced at today's higher rates.

We think a lot of the weakness in BDC prices year-to-date comes from this factor. However, BDCs were very successful in a ZIRP (Zero Interest Rate Policy) world.

VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), yielding 12.2%, is an ETF that specializes in BDCs.

Some people will see distributions declining and sell BDCs. Yet, if we look at history, when interest rates were very low in the 2010s, BIZD was paying a distribution in the ballpark of $1.50/year. Source.

What prices were BDCs trading at? Higher ones. BIZD was trading around $16-$17, with a yield of around 9%. In 2021, when BIZD had the lowest distributions it has ever had for a full year, BIZD was trading well into the $17s, with a yield around 8%.

Lower dividends from BDCs have not historically meant lower trading prices, and the reason is simple: In a 0% interest world, a 9% yield is a very attractive investment.

In many ways, the market has gotten spoiled by high yields, where we consider +10% yields to be "average." It has been a wonderful period to be an income investor and buy up so many investments at crazy low prices and higher than usual yields. Now the Fed is cutting, and those great buying times are going to come to an end. It's just a question of how quickly it happens.

However, when looking at an investment like BIZD, we can predict that the dividends will come down to somewhere around $1.40-$1.50/year – that is a 10-11% yield at current prices. Will we be able to buy BIZD at a 10-11% yield in a world where the Fed's target rate is 0%? Probably not. History says that the market is going to increase the price until our yield is only 8-9%.

Hopefully, you are loading up on income holdings today, because the reality is that it is going to get more expensive to buy income when interest rates decline. A few years from now, when interest rates are low again, you will be wishing that you loaded up with +10% yielding investments today.

A New(er) ETF

An alternative ETF to consider is Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC), which yields 10.9%. It is a younger ETF that also invests in BDCs but is actively managed. BIZD weights its holdings by market cap, and in the BDC world, the larger companies are usually lower risk and often have better-than-average performance. However, any market has inefficiencies that can provide excess returns if they can be identified.

PBDC takes an active approach, and so far, management has been earning its keep, as PBDC has modestly outperformed BIZD since inception with small but consistent outperformance:

In theory, we like the idea of active management, and we often prefer actively managed funds over passively managed counterparts.

It's been a bullish market for BDCs since PBDC's inception, and we would really like to see how it performs in a crisis. However, the BDC world is pretty small, and the holdings between the two funds are very similar. The difference in performance is primarily due to decisions to have different weightings.

Here are the top 10 holdings of BIZD: Source.

And here are the top 10 holdings of PBDC: Source.

You'll note that there is a lot of overlap but different weightings.

We consider PBDC a reasonable alternative or companion to BIZD, although we would like to see how PBDC's management performs in a crisis.

Conclusion

BDCs are a sector that has underperformed year-to-date, and we view that as an investor reaction to declining earnings and perhaps some fear of credit risk.

Yet, BDCs have traded at much higher prices in the past when earnings were a lot lower. The reason is simple: lower interest rates cause earnings to decline a little, but the value of each dividend paid by a BDC is much higher in a low-interest-rate world. So, while the dividends paid by BIZD and PBDC will decrease somewhat, they are still much higher than you will get from ETFs in other sectors. The relative value of paying more will increase as interest rates go lower.

In terms of credit risk, as we look at BDC earnings, we don't see a large increase in non-accruals. Businesses in general have been incentivized to shrink their balance sheets in a high-interest world. Every interest rate cut from the Fed makes it easier for borrowers to meet their obligations. While there will be exceptions that splash across the headlines, we view business balance sheets in general to be stable.

We'd buy BIZD first, until PBDC management proves itself through some tough times, but there is nothing wrong with buying both!

data.title: 2 Genius Companies I Desperately Want To Own
data.description:
Introduction

I have to say I was a bit surprised when I found out that Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) had bought Alphabet (GOOGL) in the third quarter. Don’t get me wrong, that’s not because I dislike the company. As I have often written, it’s one of the FANG+ stocks (or whatever you want to call them) that I like a lot, as it has proven to be able to use its massive installed base of billions of users to immediately turn AI into a profitable endeavor.

For example, using Polymarket, which is the biggest private prediction market in the world, the odds are nearly 100% that Google has the best AI model of 2025. In the first half of this year, it was uncertain, as competition was strong. Now, there seems to be no doubt.

This didn’t happen by accident, as Rihard Jarc wrote on X (formerly known as Twitter), when he noted that the giant has spent more than a decade building an edge in this field:

The reason why $GOOGL TPUs are so powerful vs the alternatives is that $GOOGL realized back in 2014 that they don't want to be beholden to $NVDA for AI inference workloads.

$GOOGL purchased 40,000 $NVDA GPUs at $130M in 2014 to power their first AI workloads. However, they quickly recognized the high cost of running those AI workloads at scale, so they hired a highly talented team to develop TPUs, focusing on making them significantly more performant and cost-effective for AI workloads compared to GPUs. - Rihard Jarc

Not buying Alphabet when I turned bullish was a huge mistake, as the company has done tremendously well since the market figured out AI wasn’t going to disrupt the company.

Berkshire has also been late to the party, which makes their latest investment so interesting. As Nir Kaissar wrote for Bloomberg, this investment is unusual, as Buffett's inner circle (we don't know exactly who had the idea) usually doesn't pay a premium.

Berkshire paid roughly 40 times trailing one-year free cash flow for Alphabet’s shares, which, for perspective, compares with an average FCF multiple of 26 times for the S&P 500 Index since 1991. (Looking at free cash flow, rather than earnings, accounts for the massive capital expenditures tech companies are pouring into AI.) That’s a bigger premium than Buffett typically likes to pay. With Berkshire’s Vice Chair Greg Abel set to lead the company in a few weeks, one gets the impression he may already be making the investment calls. - Bloomberg

I’m not saying they are wrong. What I like so much about this investment is that the conglomerate is essentially making a bet on Google’s AI success, as the free cash flow premium only makes sense if investors expect this company to remain a leader. If that is the case, I think shares will rise much further. If AI CapEx doesn’t result in high future returns, it could be dead money.

I know that what I just wrote looks a lot like "the stock either goes up or down." However, my point is that this isn’t the kind of deep value play Berkshire is known for, like buying BNSF shortly after the Great Financial Crisis, when the market hadn’t woken up to the massive potential cyclical stocks offered. Or his investments in oil and chemicals through Occidental Petroleum (OXY). In this market, buying Google is a very specific investment.

That’s the cool (and unusual) thing about this.

While I won’t compare myself to Buffett or any of the people on his team, I am also increasingly looking for unusual companies that bet on something big. LandBridge (LB), for example, is my Permian water investment with a "call option" on data centers. My investment in QXO (QXO) is a bullish bet on the housing market and Brad Jacon’s ability to build another wealth compounder.

That’s what the second part of this article is about, as I present two stocks I just put on my watchlist. Two companies I really like due to their unusual business models.

See, this isn’t a random article about two companies I like. This is about what I consider to be two outstanding investment opportunities, I really want to own, as they would make so much sense in my portfolio.

So, let’s get right to it!

#1: A Mini Version Of Waste Management At A Huge Discount

One of the biggest success stories on Wall Street is Waste Management and its peer, Republic Services, two companies that turned a dirty and boring business into massive shareholder value.

They have long-term contracts with customers like municipalities, have inflation protection through market-based pricing and CPI-linked deals, and the tailwind from anti-cyclical demand profiles. While a strong economy obviously creates more growth in waste, even during a recession, we still need companies to clean up. Below is an overview of the pricing structure of Republic Services.

As a result, these companies have done well. Waste Management, for example, has returned 15.2% per year over the past ten years. If you had invested $10,000, you would now be sitting on $45,524 if you had reinvested all dividends. Total dividend payments would have been close to $4,700, or 47% of your initial investment. Not bad, right?

The pick of this article is neither Waste Management nor Republic Services. As much as I like these companies, I found another pick, which is named SECURE Waste Infrastructure Corp. (SES:CA). The company has a market cap of C$4.0 billion, making it a dwarf compared to its bigger peers, including the Canadian Waste Connections (WCN) company.

SECURE Waste Infrastructure is also a Canadian company, which started as an oilfield service company in 2007. That’s why it has such a large footprint in the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin ("WCSB"), one of the biggest oil basins on the planet. In other words, it was basically an energy support company. If that were still the case, I would have no interest, as I already have a lot of energy exposure.

It also explains its poor stock price performance prior to the pandemic, as "nobody" cared for oil and gas exposure. When oil prices were subdued, demand for services fell rapidly.

The good news is that since then, the company has executed a major turnaround, which included the takeover of Tervita in 2021 and the divestiture of non-core assets.

Now, it has 12 industrial landfills, 10 metal recycling facilities, 3 oil pipeline systems, and 55 waste processing and transfer facilities across the WCSB and the North Dakota Bakken Basin, as we can see below.

Essentially, over the past decade, the company has gone from an energy services provider dependent on oil and gas production to a waste management company with a focus on recurring revenue streams, as 80% of its cash flow is recurring.

Even better, 75% of its EBITDA is now generated by waste management operations, which consist of processing facilities, landfills, metal recycling, and specialty chemical treatment.

Essentially, we can think of SECURE as a smaller version of Waste Management and its peers, with the major difference that it doesn’t have the massive municipal waste collection business. It’s a bit more cyclical because of these differences.

However, while the company is more cyclical, it deals with hard-to-treat and intensive industrial waste that tends to come with high margins, elevated pricing power, and, even more important, limited competition. Also, I think it’s fair to say that AI poses very limited disruption risk.

If anything, I think we can make the case that AI is a benefit, as asset-heavy companies with massive data quantities can streamline operations using increasingly advanced AI systems (as vague as that might sound right now).

Moreover, while it has higher exposure to energy through its WCSB footprint, management has done a terrific job diversifying, as we can see below:

  • It has the highest free cash flow conversion rate.
  • It has the highest margins (although the top 3 are roughly on the same level).
  • It has a supreme return on invested capital, which is one of my favorite metrics to measure how good management is at deploying capital.
  • It has a dividend yield of 2.3% (up 0.2 points since the table below was made).

Moreover, since 2023 (including 2025E), it has deployed C$1.9 billion in capital. More than half of this went to buybacks. It bought back close to 30% of its shares over the past 2.5 years, making it the most aggressive repurchaser on my radar.

It also has a healthy balance sheet with a total (not net) leverage ratio of 2.1x. Roughly 70% of its biggest customers have investment-grade balance sheets. The company itself has a BB- credit rating. While this isn’t investment-grade, it reflects its size and turnaround.

This is what rating agency Fitch had to say:

Secure is expected to generate healthy cash flow from operations. Its ability to maintain leverage within its target range depends on capital allocation policies related to organic and inorganic growth capital spend, common distributions, and share buybacks. Fitch forecasts leverage slightly differently from management, expecting it to be around 2.0x range in the near term and rise modestly over the medium term while remaining within the company's stated target range of 2.0x to 2.5x. - Fitch

With regard to its valuation, as of October 30, it traded at slightly more than 9x EBITDA, below its peer average of 14.0x. A re-rating to its lowest peer (not the peer average) would imply a 20% return. This means it still trades as an energy services company.

The only problem I have with SECURE is that oil-related services are already covered in my portfolio through LandBridge (LB) and Texas Pacific Land (TPL). Although SECURE is very different, I would be adding another company that the market still somewhat values as an energy company. While this creates long-term opportunities, it wouldn’t help my diversification. That’s the only thing I’m struggling with a bit.

#2: A Very Unique Landowner I Cannot Stop Thinking About

I love landowners. I have close to 30% of my entire portfolio invested in them through LandBridge and Texas Pacific. This company is also a landowner, yet very different, as I’m talking about Copart (CPRT), which has nothing to do with the energy industry.

Copart is a company I brought up for the first time in 2018. Unfortunately, I never pulled the trigger, as it has nearly tripled since then. With that said, Copart is a major landowner, as it owns 281 operating facilities, including in every U.S. state, in most Canadian provinces, in the United Kingdom, Brazil, Ireland, the UAE, Oman, and Bahrain. The overwhelming majority of these properties are owned (instead of leased).

So far, I haven’t told you what these facilities are all about. Essentially, they are scrapyards.

The company deals with millions of cars every year, but it doesn’t run traditional scrapyards, nor does it sell cars the way used-car dealers do. Copart works with insurance companies. It tows, stores, and auctions totaled cars on its digital platform.

They get paid fees for these operations and receive a fee from the buyer (auction, gate, and loading fees). This makes it very asset-light, as 85% of its revenues in the 2025 fiscal year came from service revenues. Only 15% came from vehicle sales. This allowed it to generate a net margin of 33%. Or to put it differently, 33 cents of every revenue dollar goes straight to its bottom line.

It has other benefits, including the fact that nobody wants a junkyard in their neighborhood, let alone one that has 50 acres or more. When adding strict zoning laws, the company gets an even bigger moat. Moreover, the fact that they own roughly 90% of their land means that they are protected against rent inflation.

Moreover, it basically has a duopoly with IAA, which isn’t a public company anymore. However, when it was still public, CPRT trumped it in terms of growth and margins.

Furthermore, inflation benefits CPRT, as the value of a totaled car rises. Bear in mind that a car is only ever financially totaled. In other words, if it’s too expensive to fix, it’s a totaled vehicle. However, in other places around the globe, people happily fix these cars, as they have lower wages (among other factors).

Given the rising age of the average car, parts, in general, become much more valuable.

Moreover, cars are becoming increasingly complex. While one might make the case that autonomous cars reduce the accident frequency, we are still very far away from that scenario. Moreover, because cars are increasingly complex, they are totaled much more easily. Just think about the many sensors, new forging techniques, and so much more.

Crashing a 20-year-old F-150 truck is very different from doing the same with a modern Tesla. The loss ratio could potentially hit 30% over time.

Also, because Copart has an increasingly large international footprint and effective digital marketplaces, it sold roughly 39% of its vehicles to international buyers. This footprint makes it attractive for insurance companies, as the odds of financially salvaging vehicles are better.

That’s the network effect, which keeps growing every year.

Essentially, it’s a bit like Facebook or Instagram. The only reason they are so popular is that "everyone" is using them. Users know they will find endless entertainment, while contributors, or whatever you want to call them, know that they can reach a big audience. Meanwhile, advertisers know their money will likely be spent well.

This is what Morningstar wrote, which gives CPRT a Wide Moat rating:

Copart’s two-sided network creates a fly wheel effect. Essentially, sellers benefit from a wide buyer network due to increased bidding competition, and buyers benefit from higher vehicle volumes. From the seller’s point of view, more potential buyers translates into more bids and thus higher selling prices. Better auction liquidity and selling prices subsequently entices sellers to pass more vehicle volume to Copart. As such, Copart’s growing size means it can procure or have access to more vehicles to sell on consignment than a small player. - Morningstar

Moreover, it’s still founder-led, as the head of the Board of Directors is Willis Johnson, who founded the company in 1982. He also wrote the book "Junk to Gold."

Adding to that, I believe the company is in a prime spot to benefit from AI. While it needs to be seen just how effective adoption will be (I’m being rather creative here), I think capabilities can be used to quickly scan and assess incoming cars, show insurance companies the potential demand for vehicles (it could help them declare a vehicle as totaled), and to streamline auction processes.

Last but not least, although CPRT doesn’t pay a dividend (I’m assuming some of my readers will hate that), it has some of the most prudent management I’ve ever seen.

  • The company has zero gross debt. Nada. Its $883 million in liabilities consists almost entirely of accounts payable, operating lease liabilities, and taxes.
  • It holds $2.8 billion in cash and $2.0 billion in investments (held to maturity securities).

This is $4.8 billion in cash and cash equivalents, or 12.3% of its market cap.

In FY26, it generated $1.7 billion in operating income and $180 million in interest income. That’s how strong this balance sheet is.

While the market expects massive buybacks, especially given its valuation, management hasn’t made any promises yet, which I like, as they don’t buy back stock to simply improve per-share numbers. They care about the long-term, which comes with a fantastic balance sheet and a lot of opportunities for M&A, new land additions, and growth CapEx.

Valuation-wise, CPRT trades at 25.1x earnings, slightly below its long-term average of 26.3x, a number fully justified by its dominant position, pristine balance sheet, and high margins.

Going forward, analysts expect mid-to-high single-digit EPS growth in FY26 and FY27. Personally, I expect these numbers to be substantially higher if we get a bottom in cyclical growth, as it could cause used car prices to accelerate, total vehicle demand to grow, and potentially even increase the traffic accidents.

In general, I am super bullish on its future due to the growing moat advantage, increasing total loss frequency, and the related surge in car tech complications.

CPRT is high on my watchlist, as I think it would look terrific next to my other holdings.

Takeaway

I thought a bit about this and think we can make the case that some of the best investments can be highly unusual ones, which is why I brought up Berkshire buying Alphabet. It’s not a traditional Buffett-like idea but a big investment in the future of AI and Alphabet’s leadership.

That’s why I like SECURE and Copart so much. They have very unique business models, wide moats (really wide!), terrific balance sheets, industry-leading metrics, and great valuations.

While neither is great for high-income investors, SECURE has a decent yield, and CPRT has a profile that should make it a great total return stock.

I would like to own both and hope to incorporate at least one of them into my portfolio over the next 1-3 months.

Risks To My Thesis

SES needs to be re-rated in order to unlock tremendous potential. This requires flawless execution and a further move into defensive areas like WM and RSG. For CPRT, the biggest risk would be that autonomous cars become so good that there are no more meaningful accidents. While that would be great for society, it’s unlikely to happen for many more decades, as this adoption is slow. Also, the combination of humans and robots always comes with accidents, just fewer.

Also, general market and recession risks apply. If the market were to take a big hit, these stocks are unlikely to be immune.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Travere Therapeutics: Thriving Kidney Franchise Set To Soar On January PDUFA
data.description:
This is my third Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) article. In my previous article, I rated Travere as a 'Buy.' It has since rocketed upwards ~107%.

Powered by FILSPARI's existing and anticipated approvals, Travere is on a tear with room to run

In 02/2023, the FDA granted FILSPARI (sparsentan) its initial approval for the treatment of primary immunoglobulin A nephropathy [IgAN]. When initially approved, FILSPARI's REMS requirement was quite severe; its objective was to:

...to mitigate the risks of hepatotoxicity and embryo-fetal toxicity associated with FILSPARI: • Objective 1: Monitor for elevations in liver enzymes in patients exposed to FILSPARI • Objective 2: Ensure that patients who can become pregnant are not pregnant before initiating FILSPARI • Objective 3: Minimize exposure in patients who may become pregnant while taking FILSPARI

This REMS was substantially eased in 08/2025 per the Important Update below:

In 09/2024, as noted in Travere's Q3/2025, 10-Q, FILSPARI garnered full FDA approval to slow kidney function decline in adults with IgAN at risk of disease progression. This important development was based on its PROTECT Study (NCT03762850), demonstrating that FILSPARI significantly slowed kidney function decline over two years compared to irbesartan.

As shown by the chart below, over the last six months, Travere has grown its market cap from a low of ~$1.53 billion to a high of ~$3.26 billion.

This reflects a startling growth of >110% in just six months. I contend that this price advancement is fully justified. Back when it was first approved in 02/2023, a Jefferies analyst estimated that FILSPARI's peak sales could reach $745 million in the United States.

It makes sense to apply a generous 5X multiple to this peak sales projection insofar as its label has since been significantly enhanced; this multiple takes $745 million to ~$3.7 billion. Thus, Travere's current pricing is not excessive; that being said, I do not think its IgAN indication alone is responsible for Travere's recent price explosion.

I believe a major contributor to excited trading in Travere is attributable to FILSPARI's potential label expansion in the treatment of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. It significantly raises Travere's potential revenue growth and thus its market cap.

On 3/17/2025, Travere filed an sNDA seeking priority review for traditional approval of FILSPARI for the treatment of FSGS. The FDA accepted the filing in due course on 5/16/2025, setting a PDUFA date of 01/13/2026. Initially, it indicated that it was planning to hold an advisory committee meeting to discuss the application.

Subsequently, on 9/10/2025, Travere issued a release that an advisory committee was no longer needed. The release noted:

The sNDA is supported by two of the largest and most rigorous head-to-head interventional studies conducted to date in FSGS, the Phase 3 DUPLEX Study and the Phase 2 DUET Study. In these studies, FILSPARI demonstrated rapid, superior and sustained reductions in proteinuria when compared with maximum labeled dose irbesartan across adult and pediatric patients. As published in the New England Journal of Medicine, DUPLEX showed statistically significant and clinically meaningful proteinuria remission at 36 weeks that was durable through 2 years.

Investors should be aware that FSGS is potentially a far bigger indication than IgAN. The excerpt below from Travere FSGS developer/competitor ZyVersa's (OTC:ZVSA) CEO makes the point:

In an April 3, 2025 analyst update on Travere, Guggenheim indicated that they believe investors underappreciate the FSGS commercial opportunity, which is substantially larger than that for IgAN. This is based on a higher unmet need for effective FSGS drug therapies since patients progress to kidney failure at twice the rate as IgAN patients, and there are fewer expected competitors reducing price sensitivity. Guggenheim has projected $2 Billion in peak sales for Filspari in FSGS. (emphasis added)

If Travere can be first in line with an effective FSGS therapy, there is no telling how high its price might go.

Despite its significant liquidity, Travere may need to raise cash to support its FSGS launch

The excerpt below from Travere's 11/2025 Corporate Overview Presentation, slide 43, pegs its liquidity at 9/30/2025 (adding in a $40 million milestone payment received in October 2025) at $195 million.

Its 10-Q lists its net cash used in operating activities for the first three quarters of 2025 at ~$23 million. Unfortunately, this $23 million was an outlier on the low side. Travere's latest 10-K illustrates its longer-term cash burn for the years 2022-2024, as shown by the excerpt below:

The $230 million burn for 2024 is an improvement over prior years but still significantly larger than its cash burn for the first three quarters of 2025. Unfortunately, management doesn't offer much help in projecting future cash needs; it ventures no cash runway whatsoever, and the only guidance it offers is set out in the following reply by CFO Kline in response to a question during its Q1/2025 earnings call:

This year we're guiding to gross to nets that are going to be in the low 20s.

While rising IgAN FILSPARI sales will help cushion Travere from liquidity shocks, I notice that 2023 had outsized cash burn. This is likely attributable to expenses incurred in connection with FILSPARI's launch, which commenced in 02/2023. Assuming FSGS is approved in January, it will likely follow suit, resulting in increased expenses, which will require additional financing in 2026.

Conclusion

I am quite bullish that the FDA will approve FILSPARI in the treatment of FSGS. I am quite sanguine about the impact of such an approval. Accordingly, I stick with my existing 'Buy' rating.

Investors should be aware that there are significant risks attendant on an investment in Travere. Most obviously, the risk of an FDA FSGS rejection or significant delay, either of which would set Travere back on its heels and likely erase its gains as shown on the chart above.

While the FDA is the most obvious concern for investors. It is hardly the only one. The 10-Q lists a grab bag of risks, most of which are recognized to some degree by biotech investors. One of these risks, which I think deserves at least passing discussion, is that Travere lacks manufacturing facilities and relies on sole-source suppliers for the manufacturing of FILSPARI and its other less significant approved product, Thiola.
